nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated, found in shallow protected lagoons and reef flats from 1 to 8 m depth. Wild individuals spend most of their time buried in sand or rubble with only the head exposed, occasionally swimming between burrows.

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and crevices

diet.notes: Piscivorous and crustacean-feeding moray. Newly introduced specimens often accept only live foods (ghost shrimp, small feeder fish) initially. Most can be transitioned over time to frozen krill, freeze-dried krill, strips of fresh marine fish, raw shrimp chunks, and squid offered on a feeding stick. Pieces should not be much larger than the eel's head. Feed every 2-3 days; targeted tong-feeding is generally advised to prevent tankmates from stealing food.

avoidWith: small fish that fit in its mouth
overly aggressive tankmates that may harass the ribbon-like tail

imageCandidates.notes: Photo (c) Gold eagle Kroll. ~1024px+ aquarium shot showing the complete slender pale white-to-cream ribbon-like body draped over live rock on white sand against a blue background; the hobbyist display view, sharp and clean, clearly showing the diagnostic pale coloration and elongated form.

Photo (c) Petr Knotek (via GBIF). Whole free-swimming individual in a natural S-curve over sand, showing the full elongated pale ribbon body; sharp, whole animal in frame, good variety vs the aquarium shot.

Photo (c) Emanuele Santarelli (via GBIF). Sharp head close-up emerging from a white-sand burrow, clearly showing the diagnostic dark facial spots and elongated tubular nostrils on a pale white specimen; kept as a non-recommended diagnostic extra (head only).

summary: The Ghost Eel is a slender Indo-Pacific moray reaching around 80 to 103 cm, with a pale white-to-cream body, distinctive dark facial spots, and elongated tubular nostrils. Generally considered an advanced species, it requires a tightly-sealed lid and patient feeding.
careNotes:
The Ghost Eel (Pseudechidna brummeri), also sold as the White Ribbon Eel and Ghost Ribbon Eel, is a slender moray from the Indo-Pacific, reaching about 80 to 103 cm as an adult. Body coloration is typically pale white to cream with tan, yellow, and dark markings; the head bears distinctive dark spots and elongated tubular nostrils. Older specimens have been reported to darken toward light brown.

This species is generally advised for advanced aquarists. A tightly-sealed lid or canopy is essential — the Ghost Eel has been known to escape through surprisingly small openings, and escape is a common cause of loss in captivity. The aquarium should include a deep sand bed of around 10 to 13 cm and PVC tube or rockwork retreats; the eel spends most daylight hours partially buried or tucked into a hide, emerging mainly to feed or after dark. It is considered largely nocturnal.

Reef compatibility is generally described as with caution. The Ghost Eel is broadly considered safe with soft corals, polyps, mushrooms, and sessile filter feeders, but will consume small ornamental shrimp and any small fish that fit in its mouth.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented in home aquaria. Unlike the closely related Ribbon Eel, the Ghost Eel is gonochoristic — meaning males and females are separate, distinct sexes rather than changing sex over the course of their lives. The larval phase is presumed to be pelagic (open-water drifting) and has not been reared in captivity.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Designated primary; product page provided care level, water parameters, diet notes, temperament, reef safety, and color description.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Muraenidae, Anguilliformes), max length (103 cm TL), wild temperature range (26.4-29.3°C, mean 28.5°C), distribution (Indo-Pacific, W. Indian Ocean to Samoa, north to Ryukyu Islands), depth (1-8 m), and habitat. Note temperature spread vs LiveAquaria; averaged in final values.

additional.name: STL Marine Solutions
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (3'4" / ~101 cm), tank size (50 gal minimum), temperament (semi-aggressive), reef compatibility (with caution), and diet/feeding behaviour. Designated secondary (theaquariumwiki.com) returned HTTP 403 Access Denied; STL Marine Solutions used as replacement secondary.

additional.name: Live Gem Factory + Frags 2 Fishes (via search aggregation)
additional.notes: Used to triangulate lifespan (reported up to ~40 years in some sources; conservative range 10-20 years used here pending firmer data) and tank-size recommendations (some sources cite 75 gal minimum, biasing the chosen 250 L).

fish.breedingNotes: Not bred in home aquaria. Males and females are separate, fixed sexes. Larvae are presumed to be pelagic but have not been reared in captivity.
